title: Nickel intercalation in epitaxial graphene on SiC(0001): a novel platform for engineering two-dimensional heterostructures
authors: Ylea Vlamidis, Stiven Forti, Antonio Rossi, Arrigo Calzolari, Carmela Marinelli, Camilla Coletti, Stefan Heun, Stefano Veronesi
abstract: Two-dimensional (2D) magnetic materials integrated with graphene offer a compelling platform for next-generation spintronic devices, yet nickel in its 2D form remains largely unexplored, due to fundamental synthesis limitations. Here, we report the controlled intercalation of Ni beneath epitaxial graphene on the Si-face of SiC(0001), achieved through a scalable colloidal nanoparticle deposition route. Chemically synthesized Ni nanoparticles (~10 nm diameter) are uniformly deposited onto graphene via immersion in colloidal solution at room temperature; subsequent thermal annealing at 650 °C drives intercalation, yielding well-ordered Ni islands at the graphene/buffer-layer interface with morphology dictated by annealing conditions. Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), supported by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, elucidate the atomic and electronic structure of the intercalated layers. DFT simulations further confirm the thermodynamic stability of the 2D nanostructures as a function of shape and lateral size, predicting a robust average magnetic moment of 0.9 $μ_B$ per atom. The resulting Ni-intercalated graphene on SiC constitutes a well-defined 2D heterostructure combining preserved graphene band structure with robust interfacial magnetism, stable under ambient conditions. These findings establish a reproducible, scalable pathway to engineer magnetic graphene-based heterostructures and open new avenues for their integration into spintronic architectures.
summary: This paper presents a new method to create magnetic 2D heterostructures by intercalating nickel nanoparticles into epitaxial graphene on SiC. By using a scalable colloidal deposition and thermal annealing process, the authors successfully placed Ni islands at the graphene/substrate interface. The resulting structure maintains the high-quality electronic properties of graphene while adding robust magnetism. This approach provides a stable, ambient-condition platform for developing next-generation spintronic devices.
main_problem: The difficulty in creating scalable, stable, and high-quality 2D magnetic heterostructures for spintronics, specifically the lack of reported nickel intercalation in epitaxial graphene on SiC(0001).
main_result: The researchers demonstrated a scalable pathway to intercalate Ni nanoparticles into epitaxial graphene on SiC(0001), creating a stable 2D heterostructure with preserved graphene band structure and robust interfacial magnetism.
method: The study uses a colloidal nanoparticle deposition route followed by thermal annealing, combined with STM, ARPES, XPS, and DFT simulations.
model_or_system: A 2D heterostructure consisting of nickel (Ni) islands or layers intercalated between the buffer layer (zero-layer graphene) and monolayer graphene on the Si-face of SiC(0001).
key_observables: Ni island height and lateral size, interlayer expansion, magnetic moment (0.9 mu_B per atom), graphene band structure, and Ni oxidation states.
important_parameters: Annealing temperatures (650-670 C for intercalation, 800 C for deintercalation), Ni nanoparticle diameter (~10 nm), and Ni-graphene interlayer distance.
main_assumptions: The Ni-graphene interaction is assumed to be weak enough that the electronic structure is nearly a superposition of isolated layers; the use of the vdw-df2-b86r functional in DFT.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Ni diffusion/intercalation schematic and STM images of Ni islands; Figure 2 provides Ni island height distributions; Figure 3 shows Ni island orientation; Figure 5 shows Ni island growth and bilayer formation; Figure 6 presents XPS spectra; Figure 7 and 8 show DFT-calculated electronic and magnetic properties.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of 2D magnetism, describes the colloidal synthesis and intercalation process, presents structural characterization via STM and XPS, details the electronic and magnetic properties through ARPES and DFT, and concludes with the implications for spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
